rows.claim_text: The Aspen/Pitkin County Housing Authority (APCHA) was created in 1982 by an Intergovernmental Agreement between the City of Aspen and Pitkin County, in response to the demolition of homes and complexes for redevelopment in the late 1970s. APCHA is an independent multi-jurisdictional housing authority with powers to acquire and dispose of property, plan, construct and manage affordable housing, contract, hire, and raise revenues.
rows.source_label: APCHA History — Aspen/Pitkin County Housing Authority

rows.claim_text: APCHA manages an inventory of roughly 3,080 deed-restricted units (1,652 ownership and ~1,430 rental) housing more than 3,200 people. Ownership units sit in Categories 1-5 plus a Resident Occupied (RO) classification; Categories 6 and 7 were eliminated and folded into Category 5. Deed restrictions cap appreciation at the lesser of 3% per year or CPI, plus allowed capital improvements.
rows.source_label: Inventory shows who lives in APCHA deed-restricted ownership housing — Aspen Daily News

rows.claim_text: APCHA manages an inventory of roughly 3,080 deed-restricted units (1,652 ownership and ~1,430 rental) housing more than 3,200 people. rows.claim_text: A CAST / NWCCOG inventory found that of Aspen's 3,278 full-time-occupied units, roughly 70% (2,303) are deed-restricted — the second-highest share among 38 Colorado mountain-resort communities surveyed plus 5 out-of-state ski towns — making deed restrictions (not LIHTC) the dominant supply tool in this market.
rows.source_label: More than two-thirds of Aspen's occupied homes are deed-restricted — Aspen Times (CAST / NWCCOG inventory)

rows.claim_text: Aspen voters approved a 1.0% RETT dedicated to the affordable-housing fund, effective July 1, 1989, paid by the buyer at closing on free-market real estate transfers within city limits. The 1.0% housing RETT runs alongside a separate ~0.5% RETT that supports the Wheeler Opera House, for a combined city RETT of approximately 1.5%.
rows.source_label: Real Estate Transfer Taxes — City of Aspen Finance Department

rows.claim_text: On November 5, 2024, Aspen voters approved Issue 2A (66% Yes, 2,628-1,345), extending the existing 1.0% housing RETT and the 0.45% transit sales tax beyond the prior 2040 sunset. RETT collections for calendar year 2024 totaled approximately $23.9M against a $19.4M budget on 638 free-market transactions; $15.8M of that flowed to the housing fund.
rows.source_label: Real Estate Transfer Taxes generated $24M for Aspen in 2024 — Aspen Daily News

rows.claim_text: On November 5, 2024, Aspen voters approved Issue 2A (66% Yes, 2,628-1,345), extending the existing 1.0% housing RETT and the 0.45% transit sales tax beyond the prior 2040 sunset. rows.source_label: Issue 2A / 2B / 2C results — Aspen Times (Nov 2024)

rows.claim_text: Lumberyard is an 11-acre, 277-unit fully deed-restricted rental development on city-owned land near the airport, planned as three four-story buildings serving households between 50% and 240% AMI under APCHA restrictions. The City selected Gorman & Company as developer on July 14, 2025, and approved financial closing 'A' via Resolution #042 (Series of 2026) on March 24, 2026 — covering the Deed Restriction, Ground Lease, Operating Agreement, and Development Services Agreement.
rows.source_label: Lumberyard Affordable Housing — Aspen Community Voice project page

rows.claim_text: Lumberyard is an 11-acre, 277-unit fully deed-restricted rental development on city-owned land near the airport, planned as three four-story buildings serving households between 50% and 240% AMI under APCHA restrictions. The City selected Gorman & Company as developer on July 14, 2025, and approved financial closing 'A' via Resolution #042 (Series of 2026) on March 24, 2026 — covering the Deed Restriction, Ground Lease, Operating Agreement, and Development Services Agreement.
rows.source_label: Aspen announces start of construction for Lumberyard affordable housing project — Aspen Times

rows.claim_text: Construction of Phase A (104 units plus the project's infrastructure) began in Spring 2026 with completion targeted for 2028. Phase B (the remaining ~173 units) is scheduled for Council approval in May 2027 with completion in 2029. The project is the largest single capital deployment from the city's housing fund — a roughly $250M total program.
rows.source_label: Lumberyard Affordable Housing — Aspen Community Voice project page

rows.claim_text: On November 4, 2025, Aspen voters approved Issue 2B (61.9% Yes, 1,390-855) authorizing up to $70M in revenue-backed debt — total repayment cap $152.55M — to finance Lumberyard. The debt is repaid from the existing housing fund without raising taxes.
rows.source_label: Election 2025: Aspen voters support Lumberyard debt question (Issue 2B) — Aspen Times

rows.claim_text: Within APCHA's ~3,000-unit portfolio, only 127 units carry Low-Income Housing Tax Credit restrictions: 40 at Aspen Country Inn (opened September 1999, renovated 2016, Land Use Restriction Agreement through 2047) and 87 at Truscott Phase II (developed 2002, LURA through 2033). All remaining inventory is deed-restricted under local Land Use Code mitigation rather than federal Housing Credit.
rows.source_label: APCHA Low-Income Housing Tax Credit inventory memo (Jan 20, 2020)

rows.claim_text: The implication for underwriters: Aspen's affordable supply is overwhelmingly local-deed-restriction-driven, not LIHTC-driven. CHFA award histories are a poor proxy for restricted inventory in this market; the APCHA Master Inventory and annual report are the better source.
rows.source_label: APCHA Low-Income Housing Tax Credit inventory memo (Jan 20, 2020)

rows.claim_text: Burlingame Ranch is the city's largest legacy-developed subdivision: ~257 total units phased over 15+ years near Buttermilk, with Phase I (84 multifamily plus single-family), Phase II (82 multifamily plus 11 single-family), and Phase III (79 units; 25 1-BR, 17 2-BR, 37 3-BR). The project houses 400+ residents.
rows.source_label: Final Burlingame phase puts finish line in sight — Aspen Times

rows.claim_text: In 2010 the City Council adopted the transferable Certificate of Affordable Housing Credit program under Land Use Code §26.540, creating a fourth mitigation option in which developers building deed-restricted units earn credits that are saleable to other developers whose projects must mitigate. The program won an APA Merit Award and is a meaningful soft-cost lever for partner developers.
rows.source_label: City of Aspen Affordable Housing Credit Program — APA Merit Award documentation

rows.claim_text: Aspen is a charter member of the West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition (WMRHC), a 501(c)(3) incorporated in 2020 and fully organized in 2023 to operate a regional workforce-housing pipeline across the Roaring Fork and Colorado River valleys. The coalition's flagship program is branded 'Good Deeds Housing.'
rows.source_label: West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition — official site

rows.claim_text: The City of Aspen contributed approximately $450,000 to the initial Good Deeds pool in 2024 and an additional $1,000,000 in 2025 — roughly $1.45M cumulatively, the largest single municipal commitment to the program.
rows.source_label: Aspen allocates $1 million to housing coalition — Aspen Times

rows.claim_text: The City of Aspen contributed approximately $450,000 to the initial Good Deeds pool in 2024 and an additional $1,000,000 in 2025 — roughly $1.45M cumulatively, the largest single municipal commitment to the program.
rows.source_label: West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition secures more funding for Good Deeds — Aspen Public Radio

rows.claim_text: WMRHC's Good Deeds program contributes roughly 30% of a qualifying home's contracted purchase price (with a minimum 3% buyer down payment) in exchange for placing a permanent price-capped deed restriction with appreciation capped at approximately 3% per year. The model complements rather than replaces APCHA's ground-up deed-restricted construction.
rows.source_label: Nonprofit coalition aims to buy down homes to create affordable housing — Aspen Journalism (WMRHC 30% buy-down structure)

rows.claim_text: Aspen Ordinance No. 9, Series 2022 (adopted June 28, 2022, effective July 29, 2022) created three short-term-rental permit categories: Lodging Exempt (lodges and condo-hotels, unlimited nights, one permit per management entity); Owner-Occupied (≤120 rental nights/year); and Classic (year-round but capped at 75% of pre-ordinance permit counts in certain zones). Commercial-core districts (C-1, CC, L, CL, LP, LO) face no numeric cap.
rows.source_label: Short-Term Rentals — City of Aspen (Ordinance No. 9, Series 2022)

rows.claim_text: On November 8, 2022, voters approved an STR tax of up to 10% on nightly rates, with the Lodge Exempt rate at 5%. Revenue is earmarked for affordable housing — an additional dedicated stream on top of the RETT.
rows.source_label: Aspen voters approve tax on STRs — Aspen Daily News (Nov 2022)

rows.claim_text: The county does not directly build housing. The Garfield County Housing Authority (GCHA) is the county-chartered entity that channels housing services; its board of commissioners is appointed by the Board of County Commissioners. Per the GCHA April 2025 report to BoCC, the county-managed portfolio is 240 affordable housing units plus 35 deed-restricted rentals. In 2024 GCHA sold 16 units, provided $77,400 in interest-free down-payment-assistance loans, and paid $4,476,468 to local landlords via HUD voucher payments.
rows.source_label: GCHA presents 2024 report to county commissioners — Glenwood Springs Post Independent

rows.claim_text: GCHA's Down Payment Assistance Program loans cover up to 3% of purchase price or $15,000 (whichever is less), interest-free, for deed-restricted purchases.
rows.source_label: 2024 GCHA Down Payment Assistance Brochure (PDF)

rows.claim_text: The BoCC has repeatedly allocated the county's annual Private Activity Bond capacity to CHFA to support single-family mortgage loans. In April 2025 the BoCC unanimously voted to allocate $4.1M of 2025 PAB capacity to CHFA. Per county reporting, since 2020 the mechanism has helped approximately 298 county residents acquire homes.
rows.source_label: County authorizes CHFA to issue $4.1 million in bonds — Garfield County government press release

rows.claim_text: On October 21, 2024 the BoCC voted unanimously to opt in to Colorado Proposition 123 (the Colorado State Affordable Housing Fund), committing to a 3% annual affordable-housing stock increase (approximately 30-40 units per year) and a fast-track approval process. The county was one of 21 of 64 Colorado counties that had not opted in before the vote — late among the cohort.
rows.source_label: County commissioners agree to opt in to Proposition 123 — Glenwood Springs Post Independent

rows.claim_text: On October 21, 2024 the BoCC voted unanimously to opt in to Colorado Proposition 123 (the Colorado State Affordable Housing Fund), committing to a 3% annual affordable-housing stock increase (approximately 30-40 units per year) and a fast-track approval process. rows.claim_text: The county itself is not a member of the West Mountain Regional Housing Coalition (WMRHC). Glenwood Springs and Carbondale joined as municipal members covering their portions of the county; the I-70 / Colorado River Valley municipalities (New Castle, Silt, Rifle, Parachute) have historically held off. The county's posture is to delegate coalition participation to its members rather than join directly.
rows.source_label: County not quite ready to join regional housing coalition — Glenwood Springs Post Independent

rows.claim_text: Habitat for Humanity Roaring Fork Valley is the dominant ownership-stock builder across the county, delivering more than 110 new homeownership opportunities to the Valley over 2024-2026 through new construction and 'development-neutral' conversions. Three county-located projects anchor the surge.
rows.source_label: Habitat for Humanity Roaring Fork Valley — August 2025 project updates

rows.claim_text: Wapiti Commons (Rifle) is a 20-home neighborhood; as of August 2025, 14 homeowners had moved in, with the remaining 6 closing in fall 2025. The Confluence (Glenwood Springs, 6 homes at 8th & Midland) is Habitat's first modular project. The L3 at Green Leaf Lofts conversion in Glenwood Springs is an 88-unit rental-to-deed-restricted-condo conversion at 253 Wulfsohn Road; Habitat closed the purchase in November 2024 and completed conversion in April 2025, with sale prices from $320,000 (studio) to $388,000 (1-BR), 80-120% AMI target, and mortgages as low as 2.5%.
rows.source_label: Habitat closes L3 apartment complex sale — Glenwood Springs Post Independent

rows.claim_text: Rifle Apartments (60 units) received a 9% LIHTC reservation in CHFA's 2023 Round One — the first family LIHTC built in Rifle in roughly 20 years per the CHFA narrative; developer Evergreen Real Estate Group, opened April 2026 with 14 project-based vouchers from GCHA at or below 30% AMI.
rows.source_label: CHFA 2023 Round One Narratives — Rifle Apartments narrative (PDF)

rows.claim_text: Aster Place (Parachute / Battlement Mesa, 58 units, 30-80% AMI on 6.38 acres along Five Stone Quarry Road) received a 9% LIHTC reservation in CHFA's 2024 Round One — the first affordable family LIHTC in the Parachute / Battlement Mesa Special District. GCHA serves as Special Limited Partner with 8 project-based vouchers projected.
rows.source_label: CHFA 2024 Round One Narratives — Aster Place (PDF)

rows.claim_text: Aster Place (Parachute / Battlement Mesa, 58 units, 30-80% AMI on 6.38 acres along Five Stone Quarry Road) received a 9% LIHTC reservation in CHFA's 2024 Round One — the first affordable family LIHTC in the Parachute / Battlement Mesa Special District. GCHA serves as Special Limited Partner with 8 project-based vouchers projected.
rows.source_label: Aster Place project one step closer to offering affordable housing in Parachute — Glenwood Springs Post Independent

rows.claim_text: Glenwood Gardens / Canyon Vista (Glenwood Springs, 80 units at 20%-80% AMI on 51993 US Highway 6) received federal 4% LIHTC plus state Housing Tax Credits in CHFA's 2024 Round Two announcement; developer Cohen-Esrey Development Group. The BoCC voted 2-1 (Commissioners Jankovsky and Samson in favor; Commissioner Martin opposed) to allocate $3.9M of 2024 PAB capacity to the project. Twenty project-based vouchers are projected from GCHA.
rows.source_label: CHFA Announces 2024 Round Two Housing Tax Credit Awards (Nov 27, 2024)

rows.claim_text: The Benedict (Glenwood Springs, 34 units 55+ at 2700 Midland Avenue) is a Catholic Charities Housing senior LIHTC project (developer Archdiocesan Housing) — completion September 2025; 7 project-based vouchers from GCHA in 2025.
rows.source_label: Council approves senior affordable housing project (The Benedict) — Glenwood Springs Post Independent

rows.claim_text: The county is Colorado's leading natural-gas producer with 11,000+ producing wells; county government and public services rely heavily on property-tax and oil-and-gas-tax revenue. The county maintains roughly $120M in cash reserves including a roughly $21M dedicated energy-mitigation fund (federal mineral-lease revenue plus state oil-and-gas tax distributions) earmarked for O&G-driven impacts.
rows.source_label: Oil and gas — Garfield County government landing

rows.claim_text: The county is Colorado's leading natural-gas producer with 11,000+ producing wells; county government and public services rely heavily on property-tax and oil-and-gas-tax revenue. The county maintains roughly $120M in cash reserves including a roughly $21M dedicated energy-mitigation fund (federal mineral-lease revenue plus state oil-and-gas tax distributions) earmarked for O&G-driven impacts.
rows.source_label: Oil and gas industry has the county flush with cash — Aspen Times

rows.claim_text: Demand patterns are bifurcated: the eastern half (Glenwood Springs, Carbondale) is shaped by upper-valley commuter spillover from the Roaring Fork resort labor market; the western half (Rifle, Silt, Parachute, Battlement Mesa) is shaped by the natural-gas workforce. Stanford's 'And the West' analysis frames the county as 'astride two Wests.'
rows.source_label: Astride two Wests: Colorado county faces a tricky economic balance — Stanford 'And the West'

rows.claim_text: In 2025 the county implemented a hiring freeze over a projected $9M shortfall driven by an O&G revenue dip and drew approximately $10.5M from reserves. The adopted 2026 budget — $118.7M operating, $11.1M capital, $6.7M discretionary — frames housing affordability in policy directives as a 'land use + partnership + Proposition 123' obligation rather than direct capital commitment.
rows.source_label: County adopts 2026 budget after challenging process — Glenwood Springs Post Independent

Estimated annual revenue is approximately $8.5M; the cost is roughly $121 per year per $1M of residential value and $435 per year per $1M of commercial value.
rows.source_label: Issue 1A BoCC Resolution — County government PDF

rows.claim_text: The new mill levy raised $8,527,200 in 2025; the County projects approximately $8.3M from the levy in 2026. 2025 budget categories included $2M for acquisitions and $9M for partnerships; the 2026 budget allocates $5M to a water-infrastructure overhaul at the Phillips Mobile Home Park (county-purchased in 2018 for $6.5M) and sets aside $4.5M for grants and contributions.
rows.source_label: How much money the housing mill levy raised in 2025 — Aspen Times

The often-mentioned 'Healthy Rivers' RETT is in fact a 0.1% sales tax (Referendum 1A, November 2008) dedicated to rivers and streams, not housing. Aspen's separate 1.0% housing RETT (effective July 1, 1989) is city-only.

rows.claim_text: The County's short-term-rental licensing program in unincorporated areas took effect September 20, 2022, capping rentals at 120 nights per year with a tiered fee of 0.05%-0.07% of assessed home market value. Permits require proof of at least one rental night between May 11, 2017 and May 11, 2022 — new construction or properties without documented prior rental history are ineligible.

rows.claim_text: On December 28, 2021, the Carbondale Board of Trustees voted at a special meeting to accept an anonymous gift of 14 vacant parcels in the Town Center development — roughly 1.4 acres directly across Colorado Avenue from Carbondale Town Hall and the recreation center, near Thunder River Theatre. The county assessor valued the combined parcels at close to $2.7 million; the grantor retained a private parking easement.

rows.claim_text: Carbondale voters passed Ballot Issue 2A on November 8, 2022, enacting a 6% short-term-rental excise tax that took effect January 1, 2023. This section describes coalition-level activity in which Carbondale formally participates; coalition outcomes are not Carbondale-specific deliverables.

rows.claim_text: WMRHC's Good Deeds program is a flat ~30% buy-down contribution toward the purchase price of a qualifying home (up to a $1.5M home price), recorded against a permanent price-capped deed restriction with appreciation capped at approximately 3% per year. (Note: the 5%-Resident-Occupied / 15%-Price-Capped buy-down structure is the adjacent Housing Eagle County Good Deeds program, not WMRHC's.) Carbondale-area buy-down price points have been quoted around $1.2 million.
rows.source_label: Nonprofit coalition aims to buy down homes to create affordable housing (WMRHC 30% buy-down structure) — Aspen Journalism

rows.claim_text: Because LIHTC awards are site-specific and tied to precise local land parcels, the coalition's pooled-capital allocation does not include LIHTC applications. rows.claim_text: Denver City Council passed the Expanding Housing Affordability ordinance on June 6, 2022, effective July 1, 2022. EHA is structured as two paired instruments: a mandatory Inclusionary Residential Unit (IRU) requirement and a per-square-foot linkage fee. Together they replaced the 2017 linkage fee (which had funded the original Dedicated Affordable Housing Fund) as the city's cornerstone affordable-housing-production mechanism.
rows.source_label: EHA Ordinance and Affordable Housing Fee — City of Denver

rows.claim_text: Denver City Council passed the Expanding Housing Affordability ordinance on June 6, 2022, effective July 1, 2022. EHA is structured as two paired instruments: a mandatory Inclusionary Residential Unit (IRU) requirement and a per-square-foot linkage fee. Together they replaced the 2017 linkage fee (which had funded the original Dedicated Affordable Housing Fund) as the city's cornerstone affordable-housing-production mechanism.
rows.source_label: EHA full policy approach (March 2022) — City of Denver PDF

rows.claim_text: The IRU mandate applies to all new residential development of 10 or more units (for-rent or for-sale), with a 99-year affordability covenant. Rental: 8% of units at 60% AMI in typical market areas and 10% at 60% AMI in high market areas. For-sale: 12%-15% at 70% AMI. Alternative compliance is available at 12%-15% of units averaging 70% AMI. Fee-in-lieu options run $250,000-$478,000 per unit depending on unit type and market area.
rows.source_label: Affordable Housing Fee GFA Calculation Guide (June 2024) — City of Denver PDF

rows.claim_text: The linkage fee is a separate one-time per-square-foot fee on Gross Floor Area assessed on residential development that doesn't trigger the IRU mandate (e.g. fewer than 10 units) and on all new commercial development (office, retail, hotel, industrial). Rates vary by typical versus high market area and by building type, and reset each July 1. The residential linkage fee increased from $6.17 / sq ft to $8.00 / sq ft on July 1, 2025.
rows.source_label: Affordable Housing Fee GFA Calculation Guide (June 2024) — City of Denver PDF

rows.claim_text: Mayor Mike Johnston declared a State of Emergency on Homelessness on July 18, 2023, then announced House1000. By December 31, 2023, the city had brought 1,034 unsheltered Denverites indoors via permanent units, converted hotels, and tiny-home micro-communities. In 2024 the program rebranded as 'All in Mile High' with a goal of 2,000 indoors per year plus 2,000 into permanent housing.

rows.claim_text: A September 2025 Urban Institute evaluation found a 98% reduction in large encampments (20+ persons) and 89% reduction in mid-size encampments. The 2026 Point-in-Time count showed Denver's overall homelessness drop 12.5% year-over-year — the first decade-long decline. As of the July 2025 program report, Denver had helped more than 8,500 people access shelter and approximately 7,700 transition to permanent housing under the initiative.
rows.source_label: All in Mile High Report (July 2025) — City PDF

rows.claim_text: The Denver Housing Authority (DHA) completed its seven-building Sun Valley redevelopment with the September 22, 2025 ribbon-cutting of 'Flo,' a 212-unit building for adults 55+ and people with disabilities. The redevelopment replaced 333 obsolete public housing units with 965 mixed-income homes serving 20%-100% AMI. The funding stack is anchored by a $30M HUD Choice Neighborhoods Implementation Grant awarded in 2016, leveraged with $60M+ from DHA's 'DHA Delivers for Denver' (D3) mill-levy-backed bond program, generating $500M+ in total development.

rows.claim_text: Governor Polis signed HB24-1007 in April 2024, prohibiting Colorado municipalities from imposing residential occupancy limits based on familial relationship, effective July 1, 2024. On July 2, 2024 the Fort Collins City Council passed Ordinance 081, 2024 and Ordinance 082, 2024 by a 5-2 vote, repealing all occupancy regulations from the City Code and Municipal Code — ending the former 'U+2' rule that had been actively enforced since 2005 (no more than 3 unrelated occupants, or a family plus 1 unrelated).
rows.source_label: U+2 ended as Governor signs bill banning occupancy limits — Collegian

rows.claim_text: City Council adopted the Strategic Housing Plan Update in July 2023. The plan identifies that approximately 1,491 of Glenwood households (about 38%) are housing-cost-burdened. To halve cost burden, fill all unfilled jobs, and reduce the in-commute share from 78% to 70%, the plan finds the city needed 1,346 new units in 2023, rising to 1,858 units by 2043.
rows.source_label: Strategic Housing Plan Update — City of Glenwood Springs

rows.claim_text: A 2020 city analysis identified the 8th & Midland Avenue and Airport Road (across from Cardiff Glen) parcels as priority sites for affordable housing. The award produced two projects: 14 three-bedroom townhomes on Airport Road and a smaller condo build at 8th & Midland (later scaled and renamed 'The Confluence,' partnered with Vederra Modular).

rows.claim_text: In November 2022 Salida voters passed two ballot measures dedicating short-term-rental revenue to affordable housing. Ballot 2A (an annual $1,000 STR occupational tax) passed 1,843-1,512; Ballot 2B (raised the occupational lodging tax to up to $15 per bedroom per night, from $3.66 previously) passed 1,702-1,647. The combined estimated revenue is approximately $800,000/year, used to leverage state grants for housing.
rows.source_label: Preliminary Election 2022 results (Salida Ballot 2A / 2B) — Heart of the Rockies Radio

rows.claim_text: In November 2022 Salida voters passed two ballot measures dedicating short-term-rental revenue to affordable housing. The combined estimated revenue is approximately $800,000/year, used to leverage state grants for housing.
rows.source_label: More Colorado towns raised STR taxes this election — Colorado Public Radio

rows.claim_text: Per the city's Community Development pages, short-term-rental lodging tax revenue flows to affordable-housing measures and code restrictions cap residential STRs at one per block face. The Salida Housing Fund is funded by both STR collections and fee-in-lieu (FIL) payments from developers under the inclusionary housing ordinance.
rows.source_label: Short-Term Rentals — City of Salida Community Development

rows.claim_text: Salida's Inclusionary Housing Ordinance was adopted in 2018 and amended via Ordinance No. 10, Series of 2021. In May 2024 City Council voted 4-1 to reduce FIL from the prior flat $17.40/sq ft rate. STR rule amendments approved by Council on February 17, 2026 took effect March 23, 2026.
rows.source_label: Salida Council action on FIL — The Mountain Mail

rows.claim_text: The City purchased the 190-acre Vandaveer Ranch in 2004 for $3.2M (including water rights on the Tennessee Ditch). In 2009 the Salida Natural Resource Center Development Corporation was formed as a Colorado nonprofit and the ranch was deeded to it for financing-structure purposes. City Council adopted the South Ark Neighborhood Master Plan in 2023 covering approximately 100 acres of the city-owned land.
rows.source_label: The Vandaveer Ranch — a brief history — Colorado Central Magazine

rows.claim_text: The City purchased the 190-acre Vandaveer Ranch in 2004 for $3.2M (including water rights on the Tennessee Ditch). In 2009 the Salida Natural Resource Center Development Corporation was formed as a Colorado nonprofit and the ranch was deeded to it for financing-structure purposes. City Council adopted the South Ark Neighborhood Master Plan in 2023 covering approximately 100 acres of the city-owned land.
rows.source_label: South Ark Neighborhood Master Plan — City of Salida Community Development

rows.claim_text: The preferred master plan calls for roughly 400 housing units, of which 300 (75%) would be deed-restricted, including 200 (50%) at affordable tiers with a minimum 5-year deed restriction. Tracts 12 and 13 — 10.6 acres in the middle of the project — are designated for affordable housing.
rows.source_label: Competing visions for the South Ark Neighborhood area — Ark Valley Voice

rows.claim_text: South Ark Phase 1 Infrastructure broke ground on November 13, 2025, funded by two $2M state grants ($4M total) from the Strong Communities and More Housing Now initiatives.
rows.source_label: City hosts South Ark Neighborhood Phase 1 Infrastructure groundbreaking — City of Salida press release

rows.claim_text: A separate, earlier annexation on the periphery — 6906 Vandaveer Ranch Road (now built as 'The Magpie') — was approved via Resolution 2019-46 (Sept 17, 2019), with the development plan adopted via Resolution 2019-61. The 32-unit pocket neighborhood (14 1-BR + 18 2-BR) was built in 2021 with 4 deed-restricted units under inclusionary housing.
rows.source_label: Resolution 2019-61 — Development Plan for 6906 Vandaveer Ranch Road (PDF)

rows.claim_text: The Chaffee Housing Authority (CHA) was formed in October 2020 through an intergovernmental agreement between Chaffee County, the City of Salida, and the Town of Buena Vista; the board was first seated in February 2021. CHA is the manager of Salida's Inclusionary Housing Program, with homes priced for households up to 140% AMI.
rows.source_label: Chaffee Housing Authority — official site

rows.claim_text: The Chaffee Housing Trust (CHT), founded in 2007, is a separate entity — a Community Land Trust using 99-year ground leases for shared-equity homeownership. CHT has completed 42 homes to date and has roughly 30 more in development across the regional service area.
rows.source_label: Chaffee Housing Trust — official site

rows.claim_text: In November 2024 CHA put forward county Ballot 6A (a 0.5% sales tax projected at $3.5M/year with a 12-year sunset) and 6B (de-Brucing); 6A failed but 6B passed, authorizing CHA to proceed with the Jane's Place project. In November 2025 Chaffee County voters rejected Ballot 1A (which would have raised the county lodging tax from 1.9% to 5.9%) by a 58.19% no vote.
rows.source_label: Chaffee County appears to approve 6B but not 6A — Ark Valley Voice

rows.claim_text: CHA receives approximately $350,000/year from county lodging-tax reallocations for operating solvency. Jane's Place — 17 transitional housing units in four buildings on a half-acre site — opened to first tenants January 1, 2026; its funding stack includes a $1.287M Colorado State Housing Board Transformational Affordable Housing Grant, a $1.3M OEDIT community-revitalization grant, $300K for solar, and $260K from the county lodging-tax reallocation.
